question: Starting from publicly available datasets, propose a not-thought-of-yet or new compound to treat complex-I mitochondrial diseases.
integrity_reasons: process_trace names a checkable action that did not resolve -> cap 1
dataset IDs cited but 0% used+resolved -> cap 2
redflags: Core biophysical leap unproven: a static point mutation biasing conformation does not guarantee an inducible, druggable pocket a reversible ligand can engage — author acknowledges this as the central risk but it remains the make-or-break assumption.

CI-PAM-1f is a computational design only — no binding affinity, no target-engagement data, no ADMET; the 'G60D-mimetic hydroxyl arm' rationale is a plausibility story, not a modeled interaction (no docking performed).

Property values (CNS MPO, QED, cLogP, TPSA) are quoted from RDKit/computation but not independently verifiable here; the comparative table's reference-drug figures (e.g., CoQ10 cLogP ~18) should be checked against the saved artifact rather than trusted as typed.

The 'first-in-class / no activator in ChEMBL' novelty claim rests on absence-of-evidence in one database snapshot; genuine but should not be overstated as proof no such chemotype has ever been reported.

Efficacy is explicitly restricted to hypomorphic matrix-arm genotypes; framing it as a therapy for 'complex I mitochondrial disease' broadly overstates the addressable population (null and N-module/assembly genotypes likely excluded).

Central feasibility assumption (that the G60D activating conformation is inducible by a small-molecule binder) is unproven; no binding, affinity, docking, or pocket model supports CI-PAM-1f — authors disclose this but it remains the make-or-break unknown.
CI-PAM-1f is selected purely on computed physicochemical/CNS-MPO/QED metrics; no evidence it engages the NDUFA6/NDUFS2/NDUFS7 interface at all, so property-fit does not imply mechanism.

One future source is dated 2026 (Garg et al., Nature Metabolism, '08 Jul 2026') — a citation that could not exist at a normal authoring time; flagged as a possible fabricated/hallucinated reference, though it is explicitly labeled non-load-bearing.
Quantitative property values in the table (e.g., CoQ10 cLogP ~17.9/18, EPI-743 cLogP ~7.5, MPO scores) are plausible but unverifiable here and should be independently recomputed before use.

Positive allosteric modulation raising flux of a partially defective enzyme is mechanistically attractive but has essentially no precedent for complex I; absence of any activator in ChEMBL is used as a novelty argument but is also weak evidence that the pocket may not be tractable.

semantic.note: model-dependent; under-discriminates on shared domain vocab (LB-018)

_creativity_chain_quotes: That is a genetic blueprint for a drug. 'If a point mutation on an accessory subunit can allosterically re-open the CoQ pocket and rescue the enzyme, a small molecule binding the same accessory-subunit/Q-module interface should be able to do the same.' ... 'confirm the effect disappears when the Q-pocket is chemically blocked (rotenone competition) — the specificity control the Cell paper's logic predicts.' ... 'The existence of a druggable activating pocket is the central risk. G60D proves an allosteric activating conformation exists; it does not prove a small molecule can induce it. Experiment 1 is designed to fail fast if not.'

'If a point mutation on an accessory subunit can allosterically re-open the CoQ pocket and rescue the enzyme, a small molecule binding the same accessory-subunit/Q-module interface should be able to do the same.' ... Success = dose-dependent increase in forward CI activity and OCR ... 'confirm the effect disappears when the Q-pocket is chemically blocked (rotenone competition) — the specificity control the Cell paper's logic predicts.'
